does: This paper introduces Generative Adversarial Distillation (GAD), a novel framework for distilling large language models from black-box teacher models using only their text outputs. GAD frames distillation as a minimax game where a student generator LLM is trained against a discriminator that learns to distinguish student responses from teacher responses. The discriminator serves as an adaptive, on-policy reward model that co-evolves with the student, providing dynamic feedback without requiring access to teacher logits or parameters. Experiments demonstrate that Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct trained with GAD matches performance of GPT-5-Chat on LMSYS-Chat benchmarks and consistently outperforms standard sequence-level knowledge distillation approaches across multiple evaluation metrics.
connects: This work builds on foundational research in knowledge distillation, which seeks to transfer knowledge from large teacher models to smaller students, and extends it to the challenging black-box setting where only outputs are available. It combines insights from adversarial training and generative adversarial networks with reinforcement learning approaches to LLM alignment, where reward models guide student behavior. The paper responds to limitations of traditional sequence-level distillation by incorporating on-policy learning, drawing from recent advances in LLM fine-tuning with learned reward models and self-play methods. It addresses the practical constraint that many state-of-the-art teacher models are proprietary and inaccessible, building on prior work exploring distillation under such constraints while improving upon previous approaches through the adversarial training paradigm.
enables: This approach opens pathways for creating smaller, cheaper models from closed proprietary teachers without requiring API rate limits or output log probabilities, lowering barriers to deploying efficient models. The adversarial framework could inspire further research into co-evolving student-discriminator dynamics and adaptive reward modeling for LLM training more broadly. Future work could explore GAD with other architectures, scaling laws for adversarial distillation, and combining it with other modern training techniques like RLHF or DPO. The on-policy nature of the approach suggests extensions toward better sample efficiency, potential applications to cross-domain distillation, and investigation of theoretical properties of adversarially-trained language models versus conventional supervised fine-tuning.
